content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 27, 1992


Frank Albino
PARKER, MILLIKEN, CLARK,
O'HARA & SAMUELIAN
Attorneys at Law
333 South Hope Street, 27th Floor
Los Angeles, CA  90071-1488

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-92-470

Dear Mr. Albino:

     You have requested advice regarding Government Code Section
87406(d)(1) of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"),¹ as it
pertains to you as a former member of the Division of Medical
Quality ("DMQ") of the Medical Board of California ("MBC").

     Pursuant to your letter, all of your questions assume that
the prohibitions of Sections 87400 to 87405 do not apply.

QUESTIONS

     1.  May you represent a physician in an administrative hear-
ing before a Medical Quality Review Committee ("MQRC")?

     2.  Between the time an accusation is filed against a physi-
cian and an administrative hearing takes place, there are com-
munications, discovery and settlement negotiations between the
attorney representing the physician and the Attorney General's
office, as counsel for the MBC.  Any proposed stipulation settling
an MBC disciplinary case must be approved by the DMQ.  May you
engage in such communications, discovery and settlement
negotiations prior to the administrative hearing?  Does it matter
if the hearing is before an ALJ or a MQRC?

     3.  Sometimes a physician who is being investigated is asked
to stipulate to licensing discipline before a formal accusation is

_____________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory refer-
ences are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Com-
mission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  ●  P.O. Box 807  ●  Sacramento, CA  95812  ●  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-92-470
Page 2


filed.  May you participate in negotiations with MBC enforcement
staff and/or the Attorney General's office with regard to such a
proposed stipulation?

     4.  If you represented a physician before an ALJ and the DMQ
did not adopt the ALJ's proposed decision, may another attorney in
your firm present written or oral arguments to the DMQ?  Could you
assist that attorney in the preparation of such arguments if you
did not appear before the DMQ?

                              CONCLUSIONS

     The following conclusions apply for a period of one year
after your term of office expired:

     1.  Since an appearance before a MQRC is for the purpose of
influencing a disciplinary proceeding involving a license to
practice medicine, you may not represent a physician in an
administrative hearing before a MQRC.  However, the statute
provides a specific exception which allows you to appear before an
administrative law judge only.

     2.  The prohibition in the statute is against you making any
formal or informal appearance or any oral or written communication
before any state administrative agency which you represented.
Therefore, this applies to any communications, discovery and
settlement negotiations prior to the administrative hearing which
are directed to the MBC, or any divisions thereof.  In addition,
you may not make any appearances or communicate with the Attorney
General's office, since that office represents the MBC.

     3.  You may not participate in negotiations with the MBC
enforcement staff or the Attorney General's office with regard to
a proposed stipulation.

     4.  The prohibition only applies to you as a former member of
the DMQ.  Therefore, you may assist another attorney in your law
firm who may present written or oral arguments to the DMQ.

                              FACTS

     You are an attorney and a former member of the Division of
Medical Quality of the MBC.  Your term of office on the MBC
expired on July 31, 1992.

     The DMQ is the division of the MBC that enforces the
California Medical Practice Act.  As such, the DMQ has the power
to revoke, suspend or otherwise discipline the licenses of
California physicians who violate that act and makes quasi-
judicial decisions in licensing disciplinary cases against physi-
cians.

     Your letter and our telephone conversation on October 2, 1992
outlined the procedure for a MBC disciplinary proceeding.  The
procedure is conducted as follows:

Our File No. A-92-470
Page 3


     1.  After investigation by MBC investigators and a determination by the MBC Chief of Enforcement that cause for disciplinary action exists, a formal accusation is filed against the physician.

     2.  If a proceeding is not settled, it is referred for initial hearing either to a Medical Quality Review Committee ("MQRC") or to an Administrative Law Judge ("ALJ").

     3.  The MQRC or ALJ conducts a quasi-judicial administrative hearing pursuant to the Administrative Procedure Act, in which the MBC is represented by the Attorney General's office and the charged physician is represented by private counsel. The MQRC or ALJ makes findings of fact and conclusions of law and issues a proposed decision.

     4.  The DMQ reviews the proposed decision for adoption or nonadoption.

     5.  If the DMQ votes to nonadopt the proposed decision, it must review the transcript and make its own decision. The parties may present written and oral arguments, based upon the record, to the DMQ. The DMQ makes a decision, which is appealable to the Superior Court.

     You would like to represent physicians who have been charged with violations of the Medical Practice Act in connection with MBC disciplinary proceedings.

ANALYSIS

     Government Code Section 87406(d)(1) provides:

          No designated employee of a state administrative agency, any officer, employee, or consultant of a state administrative agency who holds a position which entails the making, or participation in the making, of decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest, and no member of a state administrative agency, for a period of one year after leaving office or employment, shall, for compensation, act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person, by making any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, before any state administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which he or she worked or represented during

Our File No. A-92-470
Page 4


        the 12 months before leaving office or employ-
ment, if the appearance or communication is made
for the purpose of influencing administrative
action, or influencing any action or proceeding
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or
revocation of a permit, license, grant, or
contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or
property.  For purposes of this paragraph, an
appearance before a state administrative agency
does not include an appearance in a court of law,
before an administrative law judge, or before the
Worker's Compensation Appeals Board.  The
prohibition of this paragraph shall only apply to
designated employees employed by a state
administrative agency on or after January 7,
1991.

                                    (Emphasis added.)

        This provision applies to you as a former member of the DMQ.
The DMQ has the power to revoke, suspend or otherwise discipline
the licenses of physicians who are in violation of the Medical
Practice Act.  Since you would like to represent physicians in
MBC disciplinary actions, you would like to know under what
circumstances the statute applies.  The one year prohibition
applies to your questions as follows:

        1.  Since an appearance before a MQRC is for the purpose of
influencing a disciplinary proceeding involving a license to
practice medicine, you may not represent a physician in an
administrative hearing before a MQRC.  However, the statute
provides a specific exception which allows you to appear before
an administrative law judge only.

        2.  The prohibition in the statute is against you making any
formal or informal appearance or any oral or written
communication before any state administrative agency which you
represented.  Therefore, this applies to any communications,
discovery and settlement negotiations prior to the administrative
hearing which are directed to the MBC, or any divisions thereof.
In addition, you may not make any appearances or communicate with
the Attorney General's office, since that office represents the
MBC.

        3.  You may not participate in negotiations with the MBC
enforcement staff or the Attorney General's office with regard to
a proposed stipulation.

        4.  The prohibition only applies to you as a former member
of the DMQ.  Therefore, you may assist another attorney in your
law firm who may present written or oral arguments to the DMQ.

Our File No. A-92-470
Page 5


     We trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this mat-
ter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.2

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By: Jill Stecher
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JS:dl




_________________________
     2
     Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.  Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
